title: Deep Active Inference Agents for Delayed and Long-Horizon Environments

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Deep Active Inference (DAIF), an active inference agent that integrates a differentiable policy into a world model and learns a multi-step latent transition that directly predicts observations H steps ahead. By conditioning the transition on an explicit representation of the policy and backpropagating expected free energy (EFE) gradients to the actor, the agent avoids exhaustive tree search and aims to act effectively in delayed, long-horizon environments. The method is evaluated on a high-fidelity industrial manufacturing simulator with long-horizon, delayed performance signals and is reported to outperform a DQN baseline on energy efficiency per unit.
strengths: Integrating the actor “as a function” into the latent dynamics and training a single-step, H-step overshooting transition P(s_{t+H} | s_t, π̂) to enable one-look-ahead planning is an interesting twist on world-model and AIF agents.

Using EFE gradients to update the actor within a replay-trained generative model is conceptually clean in the AIF framework, unifying perception (VFE) and control (EFE) within one end-to-end pipeline.
The approach explicitly targets delayed and long-horizon settings and proposes to eliminate per-step planning via a single gradient update every H steps at deployment.

The industrial simulation domain is challenging, with delayed and stochastic feedback, mixed discrete/continuous observations, and continual non-episodic control—well aligned to the paper’s stated motivation.
The month-long evaluation protocol with 10 replications and reporting of mean ± error bars on key KPIs indicates an attempt at robustness.
The high-level motivation and positioning vis-à-vis AIF and world-model RL are clearly articulated.
The training loop alternates model learning (VFE) and actor updates (EFE), which is conceptually straightforward and well tied to the formalism reviewed in Section 2.
Demonstrating AIF-based control on a realistic industrial benchmark is valuable for broadening the application footprint of AIF beyond robotics and perception-heavy tasks.
If the one-look-ahead, policy-conditioned transition generalizes, it could reduce planning overhead in active inference and related model-based methods.
weaknesses: The proposed “policy embedding” Π is under-specified and, as implemented, appears ad hoc (concatenation of the input with intermediate/output activations for a given input), which likely does not faithfully represent the entire policy function over the horizon; this raises concerns about identifiability and generalization when π changes during training.

The single-step H-overshoot model conflates action sequence effects with a fixed-parameter policy proxy; without constraints or auxiliary supervision, the transition could overfit to specific states/actions seen during training, limiting extrapolation to new conditions or updated actors.
The claim that the method “naturally scales to continuous actions” is not backed by experiments; the main environment seems to use discrete switching actions.

Baselines are limited: there is no comparison to strong world-model agents (e.g., Dreamer/Plan2Explore/EfficientZero-like variants) or prior AIF implementations (e.g., MCTS-based or hybrid-horizon AIF), despite positioning against these lines of work.

The month-long evaluation is performed on the “best-performing instance” selected from several random initializations, which introduces selection bias. Reporting results averaged across all instances/seeds is necessary.
No ablations: there is no study of the effect of H, the policy-embedding design, dropout-based epistemic estimates, replay usage, or removing/altering particular EFE terms.
Computational cost and sample efficiency are not quantified versus planning-heavy or rollout-based baselines; the overhead of Monte Carlo sampling for EFE estimation is not measured.
Inconsistencies and typos in notation and algorithmic steps (e.g., repeated lines, unclear use of symbols φ vs Ψ for preference mapping, multiple μ/σ definitions) hinder reproducibility.
The figure caption and text disagree on the horizon (H = 100 vs H = 300); this should be reconciled.
The definition of the preference mapping Ψ and the exact components of the EFE terms used in practice are not sufficiently specified in the main text.
No direct empirical comparisons to Dreamer-family agents or other model-based methods that also handle long horizons via latent rollouts and value gradients.

Work on action-sequence-conditioned models or “macro-dynamics” (e.g., sequence-to-one transitions or policy-conditioned models via hypernetworks) is not discussed; these could be highly relevant to the proposed H-step transition idea.
reviews.questions:
1. How exactly is the policy embedding Π(Q_{φ_a}) → π̂ constructed during training and deployment? Please specify the dimensionality, which activations are concatenated, how input-dependence is handled, and why this embedding is expected to represent the full policy function over the horizon rather than a local input-output snapshot.
2. Does the transition P_{θ_s}(s_{t+H} | s_t, π̂) generalize to unseen or updated policies (i.e., new φ_a) at test time? Please provide experiments where the actor is changed after model training to assess robustness of the policy-conditioned dynamics.
3. Can you report results averaged over all environment initializations/seeds for the month-long evaluation (not just the best instance), along with confidence intervals, to remove selection bias?
4. Please add comparisons to strong model-based baselines (e.g., Dreamer variants) and to prior AIF agents (MCTS/hybrid horizon). How does DAIF’s compute time and sample efficiency compare?
5. There is an inconsistency regarding the horizon (H = 100 vs H = 300 in text and figures). Which horizon(s) were used where, and how sensitive are results to H?
6. What is the precise form of the preference mapping Ψ used to compute the extrinsic term in the EFE? How sensitive are results to this choice, and is Ψ domain-specific?
7. The algorithmic pseudocode includes duplicated lines and ambiguous symbols (φ vs Ψ; multiple μ/σ definitions). Could you provide a clean, unambiguous algorithm and release code or pseudo-code that matches the implementation?
8. Please quantify the variance of EFE gradient estimates (with and without dropout) and any regularization/stabilization used to ensure reliable policy updates.
9. Can you show ablations removing policy-conditioning from the transition and/or replacing it with sequential latent rollouts to isolate the benefits of the proposed one-look-ahead design?
10. You claim natural scalability to continuous actions. Could you include a continuous-control experiment (or a mixed-action variant) to substantiate this claim?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles an important problem—making AIF practical in delayed, long-horizon settings—by proposing an innovative policy-conditioned, one-look-ahead world model trained via VFE/EFE. The industrial control experiments are compelling in motivation and suggest potential benefits. However, the current empirical evidence is not yet sufficient for a top-tier venue: strong baselines are missing, the month-long evaluation has selection bias, and there are several clarity issues and algorithmic ambiguities (especially around the policy embedding and the EFE computation). The central idea is promising and could be impactful if validated more thoroughly. I encourage the authors to strengthen the experimental comparisons, remove evaluation biases, clarify and justify the policy-embedding mechanism (possibly with a more principled design, e.g., hypernetworks or learned function codes), and provide ablations and compute analyses. In its current form, I lean toward rejection, but I see a clear path to a stronger version with expanded evaluation and clarified methodology.
